voiceover: "Jesus was just copied from pagan myths." Let's actually check the sources. First — it's not a silly claim. There really are ancient stories of gods who descend into death and return: Osiris, Adonis, the mystery cults. And Christianity spread in exactly that religious world. The parallels are real. But read the sources. The strongest critic here wasn't an apologist — it was Jonathan Z. Smith, the skeptical University of Chicago scholar, who concluded the whole 'dying-and-rising god' category is largely a misnomer built on late, ambiguous texts. Where real cases exist, they're gods bound to the seasonal cycle — dying and reviving with the crops each year, in a timeless mythic elsewhere. The Christian claim is a different kind of thing: a named man, under a named governor, Pontius Pilate, in a known city, risen once, bodily, never to die again. Mithras and Horus don't match the story at all — once you read the sources instead of the memes. And the belief is early: Paul quotes a creed in 1 Corinthians 15 dated within a few years of the crucifixion. Too early for legend. Not a myth. Something new.

lines.t: the skeptic at U. of Chicago.

scenes.kicker: A CATEGORY BUILT ON THIN TEXTS

lines.t: in a mythic 'once upon a time.'
